verses.text: Festus, the new Roman governor, arrives in the province and immediately travels to Jerusalem. Paul is still in custody at Caesarea, awaiting trial. The Greek anabaino — to go up — names the journey from coastal Caesarea up to Jerusalem in the hills. Paul’s long detention continues. Sometimes the kingdom of God advances at the pace of imperial bureaucracy.

The Jewish leaders in Jerusalem inform Festus against Paul — still seeking his condemnation after two years. The Greek hupedeixan — informed against — names a formal accusation. Their opposition has not cooled with time. Persistent hostility is part of the apostolic story. Paul’s witness has cost him not a single trial but a season of repeated ones.

The leaders ask Festus to send Paul to Jerusalem — a request that hides an ambush plan to kill him on the way. Acts 23:12-15 has already recorded a similar plot. The Lord, however, will use Festus’ reluctance (next verse) to keep Paul safe. Sometimes divine protection works through ordinary administrative caution.

Festus declines: Paul will stay at Caesarea, and he himself will go down shortly. The Greek tachu — quickly — names how soon Festus plans to return. Paul will be safe — not by a miracle, but by a governor’s convenient preference for his own headquarters. God’s deliverances do not need angels when administrators will do.

‘Let them therefore... go down with me, and accuse this man.’ Festus invites Paul’s accusers to make their case in Caesarea, on his own ground. The Greek dunatoi — ‘those who are able’ — invites the most competent prosecutors. Festus seems committed to a fair trial, at least procedurally. Paul’s case is moving inexorably toward Rome, but each step is shaped by ordinary courtroom procedure.

After ten days, Festus returns to Caesarea, takes his seat on the judgment-bema, and orders Paul brought. The Greek bema — raised platform — will reappear in 2 Corinthians 5:10 (the judgment seat of Christ). Paul who stood before the bema of Rome will one day be confessed by all who stand before the bema of God.
25:7 The serious accusations were probably those raised previously (21:27-28; 24:5-9), but the accusations were unsupported by the evidence (24:10-13).
25:7 The serious accusations were probably those raised previously (21:27-28; 24:5-9), but the accusations were unsupported by the evidence (24:10-13).

25:9 Festus, wanting to please the Jews: The governor would later state his official reason for delaying Paul’s trial and asking to move it to Jerusalem (25:17-20). His request was driven by politics, however, not justice.

25:10-11 Festus’s suggestion that Paul be tried in Jerusalem motivated the apostle’s appeal to Caesar. Paul was not afraid of death, but he objected to being turned over to a biased court intent on murder, not justice (25:7).

25:10-11 Festus’s suggestion that Paul be tried in Jerusalem motivated the apostle’s appeal to Caesar. Paul was not afraid of death, but he objected to being turned over to a biased court intent on murder, not justice (25:7).
25:12 Festus granted Paul’s appeal. This fulfilled Paul’s conviction that he must see Rome (19:21; see also 23:11; 27:24; Rom 1:13-15; 15:22-29).
25:13-22 Festus discussed Paul’s case with Herod Agrippa II (ruled AD 50–100), who had come to Caesarea to make a courtesy call on the new governor.
25:13-22 Festus discussed Paul’s case with Herod Agrippa II (ruled AD 50–100), who had come to Caesarea to make a courtesy call on the new governor.
25:13-22 Festus discussed Paul’s case with Herod Agrippa II (ruled AD 50–100), who had come to Caesarea to make a courtesy call on the new governor.

25:16 It was a fundamental principle: Roman law did not convict people without a trial. They must be given an opportunity to confront their accusers and defend themselves (cp. 19:38-39). This put Roman judicial procedure in a favorable light for readers of Acts.
25:17 I didn’t delay: In contrast with Felix, his predecessor (24:22-27).

25:18-20 These verses give Festus’s official reasons for his actions; see also 25:9. There were no criminal accusations against Paul; the objections were about their religion and centered on Paul’s claim that Jesus . . . is alive.

25:18-20 These verses give Festus’s official reasons for his actions; see also 25:9. There were no criminal accusations against Paul; the objections were about their religion and centered on Paul’s claim that Jesus . . . is alive.

25:23-27 Paul’s hearing before King Agrippa was accompanied by all the pomp and circumstance appropriate for an official royal visit. The main purpose of the hearing was for Agrippa to advise Festus on what he should write in the appeal to Caesar, for there was no clear charge against Paul, and Festus himself believed Paul had done nothing deserving death.
